column7: Typo – This is a suggested "Print Change", and may be a worthwhile Global Correction, for the correct transliteration. Targeting with the capital letter "P", should prevent "crossfire" with the finite verbal form "prajapati" !
Typo – This is a suggested "Print Change", and may be a worthwhile Global Correction as well. I am guessing a bit on this one.
column8: srhodes@snowcrest.net:Corrected 30 Apr 2026. Rest are separately handled at https://github.com/sanskrit-lexicon/csl-corrections/issues/197

column5: -ākhyā a. N. of the organ of perception
column6: -ākhyā a N. of the organ of perception
column7: Typo – Here Again is the issue of the abbreviation "a." in italics, which indicates "adjective", BUT clearly this is a Feminine Noun. Therefore, again we have a Global Correction as follows: "ā a." —> "ā a"
column8: srhodes@snowcrest.net:Corrected 30 Apr 2026. Rest of the cases are separately handled at https://github.com/sanskrit-lexicon/csl-corrections/issues/196

column7: Typo – Headword Typo !  Please note that this is a VERY GOOD correction. It follows the printed page letter by letter.

Typo – Please note that this is a suggested "Print Change". The word "avidyā" is VERY well known across Sanskrit Literature. The use of the "Hyphen" here seems to be simply a distraction. Please bear in mind that the General Usage of the negative particle "a" is WITHOUT a Hyphen. Therefore, what is the need or valid reasoning for the hyphen in this particular case?
column8: srhodes@snowcrest.net:Kept as MW wishes. No compelling reason to change.
